31 Aug – 04 Sep 2026
All Domains
Setting Classroom Rules & Diagnostic Assessment
C1-G1-S*.* — Baseline

SWBAT understand and commit to MSCS classroom rules, routines, and behavioral expectations for the academic year; complete a diagnostic assessment to determine baseline knowledge across all 9 MoE domains for Grade 1.

Student can: (1) State at least 4 classroom rules and explain why each matters; (2) Complete the diagnostic assessment demonstrating current knowledge level; (3) Set one personal learning goal for Term 1.

Welcome back icebreaker — students share one thing about their summer. Teacher introduces MSCS classroom rules using a co-created rules poster. Students contribute ideas for classroom expectations. Diagnostic assessment administered covering key concepts from prior grade. Goal-setting activity: students write one personal learning goal.

IN-CLASS:
1) Icebreaker: Two Truths and a Lie about the UAE; 2) Co-create classroom rules poster on chart paper; 3) Review MSCS class routines, expectations, and assessment policy; 4) Administer Diagnostic Assessment (30 min); 5) Goal-setting card: 'My MSCS Goal for Term 1'

DISTANCE LEARNING (Plan B):
1) Virtual icebreaker via Padlet; 2) Digital rules agreement on Google Forms; 3) Diagnostic assessment via Microsoft Forms with auto-grading; 4) Goal-setting on Flipgrid
Diagnostic Assessment (pre-designed per grade); Classroom rules poster template; Goal-setting card template; Timer; Diagnostic answer key + rubric
MoE Textbook G1 V-1; Diagnostic Assessment document G1; Chart paper & markers; Canva (digital poster); Google Forms; Padlet; Flipgrid; Timer
Decorate the classroom rules poster at home and add a drawing of yourself following one rule. Review the diagnostic topics with a family member and note 2 things you want to improve this year.
DOK 1 (Recall & Reproduction)

ELL: Diagnostic available in simplified English with visual support; rules poster with icons/symbols; bilingual rule cards (Arabic/English). SEN: Oral administration of diagnostic; extended time; reduced number of rules to learn. Gifted: Write an additional rule they think is important and justify it; set a challenging academic goal beyond grade level.

UAE Link: Understanding school rules connects to UAE's culture of respect, discipline, and community values. Cross-Curricular: English (reading comprehension on diagnostic); Art (rules poster design). Real-Life Application: Following rules is essential in all areas of life — at home, school, and in the wider UAE community.
07 Sep – 11 Sep 2026
Unit 1: Being Kind and Fair: Everyone Around Me
SWBAT identify and describe the key concepts of 'Everyone Around Me' by engaging with textbook content, collaborative inquiry, and reflective exercises aligned to MoE standards S8, S4, S9.

Student can: (1) Describe key ideas from 'Everyone Around Me' using textbook vocabulary; (2) Give at least 2 examples from the lesson or personal experience; (3) Explain why this topic matters in daily life in the UAE.

Activate prior knowledge from the previous grade through a KWL chart. Students share what they already know and what they want to learn this year. Preview the textbook, key vocabulary, and learning routines. Set personal learning goals for Term 1.

IN-CLASS:
1) KWL Chart activation — students list what they Know / Want to know about 'Everyone Around Me'; 2) Guided textbook reading p.8 with annotation strategy (highlight key terms, underline main ideas); 3) Whole-class discussion: 'Everyone Around Me' — Think-Pair-Share with structured sentence frames; 4) Vocabulary matching activity with key terms from p.8; 5) Concept mapping on whiteboard with student contributions; 6) Exit reflection: 'One thing I learned, one question I have'

DISTANCE LEARNING (Plan B):
Plan B (Distance): 1) Padlet KWL digital board; 2) Screen-recorded textbook walkthrough of p.8; 3) Flipgrid video response to discussion question; 4) Quizlet vocabulary set for independent practice; 5) Digital worksheet via Nearpod

Exit Ticket: 3-2-1 Strategy — Write 3 things you learned about 'Everyone Around Me', 2 things you found interesting, 1 question you still have. Quick vocabulary check — match 5 key terms to definitions.
MoE Textbook G1 V-1 p.8; Teacher Guide G1 V-1; Canva (creative design); Kahoot! (formative quiz); Padlet (collaborative board); Twinkl supplementary worksheets
Creative Extension: Draw and label a picture showing 'Everyone Around Me' with 3 important details. Ask a family member to share one thing they know about this topic and write it down.
DOK 1 (Recall & Reproduction)

ELL: Bilingual vocabulary cards (Arabic/English); sentence frames for written responses; visual glossary with images; audio recording of textbook passages; peer buddy system for discussion activities.
SEN: Simplified text excerpt from textbook; graphic organizers with sentence starters; reduced writing demands (oral responses accepted); extended time for tasks; multi-sensory materials (tactile maps, artifacts); frequent check-ins.
Gifted: Research extension — investigate a related topic in greater depth using primary sources; create a teaching resource for peers; design a higher-order question for class discussion; independent project connecting the topic to global contexts and UAE Vision 2031.

UAE Cultural Heritage & Tolerance: Links to UAE's vision for a tolerant, cohesive society rooted in Islamic values and national identity
Cross-Curricular: English (reading comprehension & writing); Art (creative expression); Science (where applicable)
Real-Life Application: Students connect learning to their daily lives in the UAE — understanding their community, national identity, and role as responsible residents and citizens contributing to the UAE's vision for the future.

Setting Classroom Rules & Diagnostic Assessment
C1-G2-S*.* — Baseline

SWBAT understand and commit to MSCS classroom rules, routines, and behavioral expectations for the academic year; complete a diagnostic assessment to determine baseline knowledge across all 9 MoE domains for Grade 2.

Student can: (1) State at least 4 classroom rules and explain why each matters; (2) Complete the diagnostic assessment demonstrating current knowledge level; (3) Set one personal learning goal for Term 1.

Welcome back icebreaker — students share one thing about their summer. Teacher introduces MSCS classroom rules using a co-created rules poster. Students contribute ideas for classroom expectations. Diagnostic assessment administered covering key concepts from prior grade. Goal-setting activity: students write one personal learning goal.

1) Icebreaker: Two Truths and a Lie about the UAE; 2) Co-create classroom rules poster on chart paper; 3) Review MSCS class routines, expectations, and assessment policy; 4) Administer Diagnostic Assessment (30 min); 5) Goal-setting card: 'My MSCS Goal for Term 1'

Unit 1: Respect for Difference: We Are All Different
SWBAT identify and describe the key concepts of 'We Are All Different' by engaging with textbook content, collaborative inquiry, and reflective exercises aligned to MoE standards S4, S8, S9.

Student can: (1) Describe key ideas from 'We Are All Different' using textbook vocabulary; (2) Give at least 2 examples from the lesson or personal experience; (3) Explain why this topic matters in daily life in the UAE.

Activate prior knowledge from the previous grade through a KWL chart. Students share what they already know and what they want to learn this year. Preview the textbook, key vocabulary, and learning routines. Set personal learning goals for Term 1.

IN-CLASS:
1) KWL Chart activation — students list what they Know / Want to know about 'We Are All Different'; 2) Guided textbook reading p.6 with annotation strategy (highlight key terms, underline main ideas); 3) Whole-class discussion: 'We Are All Different' — Think-Pair-Share with structured sentence frames; 4) Vocabulary matching activity with key terms from p.6; 5) Concept mapping on whiteboard with student contributions; 6) Exit reflection: 'One thing I learned, one question I have'

Exit Ticket: 3-2-1 Strategy — Write 3 things you learned about 'We Are All Different', 2 things you found interesting, 1 question you still have. Quick vocabulary check — match 5 key terms to definitions.

04 Jan – 08 Jan 2027
Unit 4: Compassion and Empathy: Showing Respect to Others
SWBAT identify and describe the key concepts of 'Showing Respect to Others' by engaging with textbook content, collaborative inquiry, and reflective exercises aligned to MoE standards S4, S8, S9.

Student can: (1) Describe key ideas from 'Showing Respect to Others' using textbook vocabulary; (2) Give at least 2 examples from the lesson or personal experience; (3) Explain why this topic matters in daily life in the UAE.
Activate prior knowledge from Term 1 through a review quiz and concept map. Students share what they remember and connect to Term 2 themes. Preview the new textbook volume and key vocabulary.

IN-CLASS:
1) KWL Chart activation on 'Showing Respect to Others'; 2) Guided textbook reading p.6 with annotation strategy; 3) Whole-class discussion — Think-Pair-Share with sentence frames; 4) Vocabulary matching with key terms; 5) Concept mapping on whiteboard; 6) Exit reflection

DISTANCE LEARNING (Plan B):
Plan B (Distance): 1) Padlet KWL board; 2) Screen-recorded textbook walkthrough p.6; 3) Flipgrid video response; 4) Quizlet vocabulary set; 5) Digital worksheet via Nearpod
Exit Ticket: 3-2-1 Strategy — 3 things learned about 'Showing Respect to Others', 2 interesting points, 1 question. Quick vocabulary check.
MoE Textbook G4 V-2 p.6; Teacher Guide G4 V-2; TED-Ed ethics videos; UNESCO tolerance resources; Character education scenario cards; Values poster template
Creative Extension: Draw and label 'Showing Respect to Others' with 3 details. Ask a family member what they know and write it down.
DOK 1 (Recall & Reproduction)

ELL: Bilingual vocabulary cards (Arabic/English); sentence frames; visual glossary; audio recordings; peer buddy.
SEN: Simplified text; graphic organizers with sentence starters; reduced writing (oral accepted); extended time; multi-sensory materials; frequent check-ins.
Gifted: Research extension with primary sources; create teaching resource for peers; design higher-order question; independent project connecting to UAE Vision 2031.

UAE Cultural Heritage & Tolerance: Links to UAE's vision for a tolerant, cohesive society rooted in Islamic values
Cross-Curricular: English (reading comprehension & writing); Art (creative expression)
Real-Life Application: Students connect learning to daily life in the UAE — understanding their community, national identity, and role as responsible citizens contributing to the UAE's future.

04 Jan – 08 Jan 2027
Unit 4: The Growth of Consultative Governance in the UAE: Majlis: The Traditional Means of Consultation

SWBAT analyze, evaluate, and synthesize the key concepts of 'Majlis: The Traditional Means of Consultation' by engaging with textbook content, collaborative inquiry, and reflective exercises aligned to MoE standards S2, S9, S4.

Student can: (1) Provide a detailed explanation of 'Majlis: The Traditional Means of Consultation' with supporting textual evidence; (2) Analyze connections between concepts with at least 2 specific examples; (3) Apply understanding to a new scenario or real-world UAE context with justification.
Activate prior knowledge from Term 1 through a review quiz and concept map. Students share what they remember and connect to Term 2 themes. Preview the new textbook volume and key vocabulary.

IN-CLASS:
1) KWL Chart activation on 'Majlis: The Traditional Means of Consultation'; 2) Guided textbook reading p.8 with annotation strategy; 3) Whole-class discussion — Think-Pair-Share with sentence frames; 4) Vocabulary matching with key terms; 5) Concept mapping on whiteboard; 6) Exit reflection

DISTANCE LEARNING (Plan B):
Plan B (Distance): 1) Padlet KWL board; 2) Screen-recorded textbook walkthrough p.8; 3) Flipgrid video response; 4) Quizlet vocabulary set; 5) Digital worksheet via Nearpod
Exit Ticket: 3-2-1 Strategy — 3 things learned about 'Majlis: The Traditional Means of Consultation', 2 interesting points, 1 question. Quick vocabulary check.
MoE Textbook G8 V-2 p.8; Teacher Guide G8 V-2; UAE Government Portal (u.ae); iCivics interactive games; UNICEF resources; Federal National Council educational materials

Creative Extension: Design a digital infographic on 'Majlis: The Traditional Means of Consultation' using Canva with 3+ key facts and 1 UAE example. Interview a family member and write a 150-word reflection.
DOK 1–2 (Recall & Skill/Concept)

ELL: Bilingual vocabulary cards (Arabic/English); sentence frames; visual glossary; audio recordings; peer buddy.
SEN: Simplified text; graphic organizers with sentence starters; reduced writing (oral accepted); extended time; multi-sensory materials; frequent check-ins.
Gifted: Research extension with primary sources; create teaching resource for peers; design higher-order question; independent project connecting to UAE Vision 2031.

UAE Cultural Heritage & Tolerance: Links to UAE's vision for a tolerant, cohesive society rooted in Islamic values
Cross-Curricular: English (persuasive writing); Islamic Studies (justice & ethics); Mathematics (civic data analysis)
Real-Life Application: Students connect learning to daily life in the UAE — understanding their community, national identity, and role as responsible citizens contributing to the UAE's future.

I can identify at least 3 roles of government leaders.
I can name examples of public servants in my community.

Week 1

Geography & History
(West Asia)
Geography of West Asia
SWBAT analyze the physical and human geography of West Asia, identify key countries, physical features, and natural resources, and explain how geography influences settlement and economy.
Ask: "What countries are in West Asia? Where is the UAE located?" Show a map and have students identify familiar countries.

Map labeling: Students label countries, seas, rivers (Tigris, Euphrates, Orontes), and mountain ranges. Physical features discussion: deserts, valleys, fertile crescent. Resource mapping: Oil and gas fields, water scarcity. Read textbook pp. 66-75. Key terms: West Asia, Fertile Crescent, Tigris, Euphrates, desalination, OPEC, strategic chokepoint.

I can identify and describe key physical features of West Asia.

Week 4

Geography & History
(The Ottoman Empire)

The Rise of the Ottoman Empire — Part 1: Origins & Early Expansion    [Content Note: Lesson expanded into two parts to support differentiated instruction and deeper engagement with complex historical narratives, per ADEK best practices for Middle School social studies and evidence-based historical reasoning.]

SWBAT identify the origins and early expansion of the Ottoman Empire, describe key events such as the Battle of Manzikert and the founding of the Ottoman dynasty, and analyze how geography enabled Ottoman growth.

[Note: Content restructured for enhanced conceptual scaffolding and analytical depth; adapted to align with ADEK Cycle 3 standards for critical historical analysis and primary source evaluation at Grade 8 level.]
Ask: "What empires have we studied? What makes an empire powerful?" Show a map of the Ottoman Empire at its peak. Quick review of West Asia and North Africa geography.

Timeline: Origins of the Ottoman Empire — Central Asian Turkish tribes, westward migration, early settlement in Anatolia. Key events: Battle of Manzikert (1071), founding of the Ottoman dynasty by Osman I (c. 1299), expansion under Orhan and Murad I. Map activity: Trace Ottoman expansion from Anatolia into the Balkans. Read textbook pp. 87-95 (Part 1). Key terms: Osman I, ghazi, Anatolia, Manzikert, sultanate, beylik.

I can accurately locate key Ottoman territories on a map.
I can explain the origins of the Ottoman Empire and its early expansion.
I can describe the significance of the Battle of Manzikert and Osman I.
Timeline Check: Students place at least 5 key events in chronological order. Map Quiz: Label early Ottoman territories. Exit Ticket: What enabled the Ottomans to expand so quickly?

Week 5
Apr 20 - Apr 24

Geography & History
(The Ottoman Empire)

The Rise of the Ottoman Empire — Part 2: Consolidation & Growth    [Content Note: Lesson expanded into two parts to support differentiated instruction and deeper engagement with complex historical narratives, per ADEK best practices for Middle School social studies and evidence-based historical reasoning.]

SWBAT describe the consolidation and growth of the Ottoman Empire, explain the significance of the conquest of Constantinople (1453), and analyze the administrative and military systems that enabled Ottoman dominance.

[Note: Content restructured for enhanced conceptual scaffolding and analytical depth; adapted to align with ADEK Cycle 3 standards for critical historical analysis and primary source evaluation at Grade 8 level.]
Review Part 1: Name 3 key events from Ottoman origins. Ask: "What happened to the Byzantine Empire? Why was Constantinople so important?"

Conquest of Constantinople (1453): Mehmed II, the siege, the fall of the Byzantine Empire. Military systems: Janissary corps, devshirme system. Administrative structure: Millet system, provincial governance. Cultural achievements: Architecture, scholarship, religious tolerance. Read textbook pp. 87-95 (Part 2). Key terms: Mehmed the Conqueror, Constantinople, Istanbul, Janissary, devshirme, millet, Hagia Sophia.

I can explain the significance of the conquest of Constantinople in 1453.
I can describe the Janissary system and millet system.
I can analyze how Ottoman administration enabled control of a diverse empire.

Geography & History
(The Ottoman Empire)

Suleyman I and His Conquests — Part 1

[Content Note: Lesson expanded into two parts to support differentiated instruction and deeper engagement with complex historical narratives, per ADEK best practices for Middle School social studies and evidence-based historical reasoning.]

SWBAT describe the peak of the Ottoman Empire under Suleyman I (Suleiman the Magnificent), analyze his military campaigns and expansion, and evaluate his leadership and legacy.

[Note: Lesson expanded into two parts to support differentiated instruction and deeper engagement with complex historical narratives, per ADEK best practices for Middle School social studies and evidence-based historical reasoning.]
Review: What happened in 1453? Who conquered Constantinople? Ask: "Who was the most powerful Ottoman sultan? What made him great?"

Suleyman I biography: Rise to power, personality, vision. Military campaigns: Belgrade (1521), Rhodes (1522), Mohacs (1526), Siege of Vienna (1529). Naval expansion: Mediterranean dominance, Barbarossa Hayreddin Pasha. Empire at its peak: Territorial extent, population, wealth. Read textbook (Suleyman section, Part 1). Key terms: Suleiman the Magnificent, Kanuni (the Lawgiver), Grand Vizier, Janissary, siege, expansion.

Week 11

Geography & History
(The Ottoman Empire)

Ottoman Istanbul & Architecture — Part 2

[Content Note: Lesson expanded into two parts to support differentiated instruction and deeper engagement with complex historical narratives, per ADEK best practices for Middle School social studies and evidence-based historical reasoning.]

SWBAT describe the transformation of Constantinople into Ottoman Istanbul, analyze the architectural achievements under Suleyman I (especially the work of Mimar Sinan), and evaluate the cultural and intellectual flowering of the Ottoman Golden Age.

[Note: Lesson expanded into two parts to support differentiated instruction and deeper engagement with complex historical narratives, per ADEK best practices for Middle School social studies and evidence-based historical reasoning.]
Review: What made Suleyman "the Magnificent"? Ask: "What do you think Istanbul looked like after the Ottomans conquered it?"

Transformation of Constantinople into Istanbul: Hagia Sophia conversion, new mosques, markets, and public buildings. Mimar Sinan: The greatest Ottoman architect — Suleymaniye Mosque, Selimiye Mosque. Cultural flowering: Poetry, calligraphy, scholarship, Turkish bath culture. Urban planning: Istanbul as a world city. Read textbook (Ottoman Istanbul section, Part 2). Key terms: Mimar Sinan, Suleymaniye Mosque, Hagia Sophia, mihrab, minaret, hammam, calligraphy, waqf.

Real-Life Application
SWBAT define civic engagement and active citizenship, and provide examples of how people contribute to their communities.
Ask: "What is a community? How do people help their community?" Discuss: What does it mean to be an active member of society?

Discussion: What is civic engagement? Examples of civic participation (volunteering, voting, advocacy). Case study: UAE community initiatives (Takatof, Red Crescent). Group activity: Brainstorm ways students can be civically engaged. Read textbook pp. 4-9. Key terms: civic engagement, active citizenship, volunteering, advocacy, community service, social responsibility, loyalty.

I can define civic engagement and active citizenship in my own words.
I can give examples of how people contribute to their communities.
I can identify at least 2 ways I can be civically engaged.
Exit Ticket: Write one way you can be civically engaged in your school or community. Thumbs Up/Thumbs Down: Identify scenarios as civic engagement or not.

UAE Link: UAE's national volunteering programs (Takatof, Emirates Red Crescent); National Service; promoting unity and social responsibility; loyalty to the nation and its leadership. Cross-Curricular: English (persuasive writing about community service). Real-Life: Civic engagement means being an active, responsible member of your community — at school, at home, and in society.
SWBAT deepen their understanding of civic engagement by analyzing different forms of civic participation and evaluating the impact of civic action on communities.
Review: Name 3 types of civic engagement from last week. Ask: "What motivates people to get involved in their community?"

Case studies analysis: 3 different civic engagement scenarios (volunteering, advocacy, community organizing). Impact evaluation: How does civic engagement change communities? Role-play: Students take on the role of community advocates and propose solutions to a local issue. Read textbook pp. 4-9 (continued).

SWBAT define what it means to be a good citizen, identify qualities such as respect, responsibility, and resourcefulness, and describe the civic virtues that strengthen communities.
Ask: "What makes someone a good citizen? Name 3 qualities." Discuss: Is being a good citizen the same as following the law?

Qualities of a good citizen: respectful, responsible, resourceful, honest, compassionate. Civic virtues: tolerance, cooperation, integrity. Scenario analysis: Is this person a good citizen? Why or why not? Read textbook pp. 10-15. Key terms: good citizen, civic virtue, respect, responsibility, resourcefulness, tolerance, integrity, cooperation.

I can identify and describe at least 3 qualities of a good citizen.
I can explain the difference between following laws and being a good citizen.
I can analyze scenarios and determine if someone is demonstrating good citizenship.
Scenario Response: Given 3 scenarios, students identify which civic virtue is demonstrated and explain why. Self-assessment: Which civic virtue do I practice most? Which do I need to improve?

ELL: Visual scenario cards with simplified text; bilingual key terms; sentence frames for analysis.
SEN: Focus on 2 civic virtues; use picture-based scenarios.
Gifted: Compare the concept of good citizenship in the UAE to another country and write a comparative analysis.

UAE Link: Sheikh Khalifa bin Zayed's emphasis on active citizenship; UAE Moral Education values; the concept of loyalty and national duty in Emirati culture. Cross-Curricular: English (analytical writing). Real-Life: Being a good citizen means contributing positively to your community and upholding shared values.
The Good Citizens in My Country
SWBAT identify the key duties and responsibilities of a good citizen in the UAE, and understand how these duties operate at the family, community, and national levels.
Review: What are 3 qualities of a good citizen? Ask: "What are YOUR duties as a resident or citizen of the UAE?"

Duties and responsibilities at 3 levels: Family (respecting elders, contributing to household), Community (volunteering, following rules, helping neighbors), Nation (respecting laws, national pride, environmental responsibility). UAE-specific duties: Loyalty, respecting laws, observing customs, contributing to development. Read textbook pp. 16-21. Key terms: duty, responsibility, family, community, nation, loyalty, customs, national pride.

I can list at least 5 responsibilities of a good citizen in the UAE.
I can categorize duties at the family, community, and national levels.
I can explain why good citizenship matters for the UAE's development.
Three-Level Chart: Students list 2+ duties at each level (family, community, nation). Reflection writing: "I am a good citizen when I..." (complete in 5+ sentences).

MSC Grade 9 Student Book pp. 16-21

Interview a family member about their civic duties. Write a short report comparing their duties to yours.

ELL: Visual duty cards; simplified chart template; sentence frames for writing.
SEN: Focus on family and community levels only; picture-based chart.
Gifted: Analyze how civic duties in the UAE compare to those in other countries and evaluate differences.

UAE Link: Demonstrating loyalty to the nation and respecting laws; observing Emirati customs and traditions; contributing to the UAE's development; national pride and identity. Cross-Curricular: English (reflective and comparative writing). Real-Life: Understanding your duties as a citizen or resident helps you contribute positively to the UAE community.
The Good Citizens in My Country (Continued)
SWBAT apply their understanding of UAE citizenship by analyzing real-world examples of good citizenship and reflecting on their own civic identity and responsibilities.
Review: Name 2 duties at each level (family, community, nation). Ask: "Who is a good citizen that you admire? What do they do?"

Real-world examples: UAE community leaders and their contributions. Profile analysis: What makes someone a model citizen? Self-reflection: My civic identity — who am I as a citizen? Group discussion: How can our class practice good citizenship? Action planning: Set a personal civic goal for the term. Read textbook pp. 16-21 (continued).

I can identify a real-world example of good citizenship in the UAE.
I can articulate my own civic identity and values.
I can set a personal civic goal and explain why it matters.
Civic Identity Profile: Students write a personal civic profile (5+ sentences) identifying their values, duties, and a specific civic goal. Peer sharing and feedback.

SWBAT describe the competencies needed for effective civic engagement (cooperation, teamwork, leadership, advocacy), and analyze real-world examples of community action.
Review: What is civic engagement? What makes a good citizen? Ask: "What skills do you need to make a difference in your community?"

Competency exploration: Cooperation, teamwork, leadership, advocacy, initiative. Case study: A community action project — how did they organize? What skills did they use? Self-assessment: Which civic competencies do I have? Which do I need to develop? Team challenge: Small groups plan a mini community action project. Read textbook pp. 22-27. Key terms: competencies, cooperation, teamwork, leadership, advocacy, empowerment, initiative, community action.
